title: He Kōrero Tuku Iho: Voices of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream | Legacy of the Waterway: Understanding Narrative Through Place
subject: English
culturalContext: Students will explore how the Mangakōtukutuku Stream (Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku) has shaped and been shaped by the stories of Ngāti Kahungunu. This includes examining traditional pūrākau (legends) of the stream, historical accounts of its significance to local hapū, and contemporary narratives about its restoration and protection. The lesson connects to the college's role as kaitiaki of this local taonga.
learningObjectives: Analyze how different narrative perspectives (Māori, Pākehā, ecological) describe the cultural and environmental significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Synthesize information from oral histories, written texts, and environmental data to construct a multi-voiced narrative about the stream.
Create a piece of persuasive writing (e.g., a speech, opinion piece, or formal proposal) that argues for the stream's protection, using evidence gathered from research and community engagement.
Present findings using appropriate oral language features, connecting the personal and local to broader themes of kaitiakitanga and sustainability.

activities.title: Whakarongo: Listening to the Awa (The Stream's Story)
activities.description: Students begin by visiting the Mangakōtukutuku Stream. They will silently observe and record their initial impressions. Back in class, they will listen to a recorded pūrākau (legend) about the stream from a local kaumātua (elder) and read a short historical extract from early Pākehā settlers describing the same landscape. They will create a T-chart comparing the language, perspective, and purpose of each account.

activities.title: Titiro: Observing Change Through Time
activities.description: Using a curated set of resources (historical photos, recent news articles, water quality data from the local council), students will work in small groups to create a timeline that tracks the environmental and social changes to the stream and its banks. They will annotate this timeline with key quotes from the texts studied, identifying cause and effect relationships.

activities.title: Kōrero: Voices of the Kaitiaki (Guardians)
activities.description: A representative from the local Mangakōtukutuku Streamcare Group will visit the class for a Q&A session. Students will prepare questions in advance about the group's work, challenges, and vision for the stream. This activity focuses on active listening and formulating insightful questions to understand a modern community perspective.

activities.title: Tuhituhi: Crafting a Persuasive Argument
activities.description: Based on their research, students will draft a piece of persuasive writing. They must choose a specific audience (e.g., the college board, the local council, a community newsletter) and a specific purpose (e.g., advocating for a planting day, opposing a potential development, promoting a clean-up event). Their writing must integrate evidence from at least three different sources explored in class.

activities.title: Whakaatu: Presenting to the Paepae (Forum)
activities.description: Students will present the core argument of their persuasive writing to a small group (their 'paepae' or forum). The focus is on using effective oral language techniques (volume, pace, tone, eye contact) to engage their audience and convincingly deliver their message regarding the protection of the awa.

resources: Audio recording of Uncle Ted Tamatea sharing the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (archived in college library).
Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'Land and Water' portal for current and historical water quality data for the Mangakōtukutuku stream.
The 'Mangakōtukutuku Streamcare Group' Facebook page and contact details for their coordinator.
The book 'Ngāti Kahungunu: The History and Culture of the Hawke's Bay People' by John C. Moorfield for historical context.

tasks: A completed multi-source research portfolio including the T-chart, annotated timeline, and interview notes.
A final written persuasive text (approx. 500 words) tailored for a specific audience and purpose.
A brief (2-3 minute) oral presentation of the written argument's key points.

nzcAlignment: English: Listening, Reading, and Viewing - Processes and Strategies; Ideas; Language Features
English: Speaking, Writing, and Presenting - Processes and Strategies; Purposes and Audiences; Ideas; Language Features
Social Sciences: Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways.
Social Sciences: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges.
